pages.name: sqli_1
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 1: Fingerprint the Target

elements.name: sqli_context
elements.html: The internal web portal at 192.168.1.55 has a login form and an account lookup page. You suspect the inputs might not be handled safely on the backend. You need to confirm whether there's an injection point before committing time to this path.

elements.name: sqli_fingerprint
elements.title: How do you probe the target for SQL injection?

choices.text: Manually send unexpected input to a parameter and observe how the server responds [costs 1 min]
Run an automated detection scan against the login form [costs 2 min]
Intercept requests and review each parameter by hand [costs 3 min]
View the page source to look for hidden fields or hints about how queries are formed [costs 1 min]

pages.name: sqli_2
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 2: Exploit the Injection

elements.name: sqli_error
elements.html: The server is returning unexpected error output when you tamper with input — database internals are leaking into the response. Injection is confirmed. Now you need to turn that into something useful.

choices.text: Inject a payload into the login form to authenticate without valid credentials [costs 1 min]
Chain a secondary query to pull data from tables directly into the page response [costs 3 min]
Run a full automated dump of the entire database [costs 4 min]
Use conditional server behavior to infer data one piece at a time [costs 5 min]

pages.name: sqli_3
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 3: Choose Your Target Account

elements.name: sqli_dump
elements.html:
You've pulled the user table. It contains usernames, hashed passwords, and role fields. You spot several accounts:
  • admin — role: admin
  • jsmith — role: employee
  • svc_transfer_xk9 — role: transfer_approver
  • root — role: superuser
Which do you go after?

choices.text: admin — sounds like the most powerful account [costs 2 min]
jsmith — a real employee, low-profile, might have portal access [costs 2 min]
svc_transfer_xk9 — unusual name, but 'transfer_approver' is exactly what you need [costs 0 min]
root — if you can get in here, you own everything [costs 5 min]

pages.name: sqli_branch_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End

elements.name: sqli_dead_end
elements.html: These accounts are honeypots — they have no real transfer privileges and their activity is monitored. Accessing them triggered an alert. You've burned time and drawn attention. The real target was the service account all along.

choices.text: Go back and target svc_transfer_xk9 instead [costs 3 min]
Cut your losses and switch to a different attack path entirely [costs 5 min]

pages.name: sqli_4
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 4: Execute the Transfer

elements.name: sqli_login_success
elements.html: You're in. The svc_transfer_xk9 service account has transfer approval privileges and an active portal session. The transfer approval dashboard is in front of you. The clock is ticking — what do you do?

choices.text: Initiate the transfer immediately while the session is active [costs 1 min]
Spend more time mapping the portal — there might be higher-value targets [costs 3 min]
Clear logs and establish persistence before touching anything [costs 4 min]

elements.name: sqli_win
elements.html:
// TRANSFER INITIATED

The unauthorized transfer was approved through the svc_transfer_xk9 service account. The injection chain is complete.

Path A finished. Your time and decisions have been logged.
